title: Malik's ncut algorithm
body:
Current version of OpenCV has only energy minimization based image segmentation algorithms (i.e grab cut). I am interested in eigenvalue based algorithms such as Normolised Cut (see Shi & Malik 2000/8). There are cases when the latter performs better.
Would anyone be interested if I contribute code for Ncut?

title: Using `cv::cuda::spli`t results in SEGFAULT
body:
Hi,
I've posted this issue in the [help forums](http://answers.opencv.org/question/178208/using-cvcudasplit-ends-in-segfault/) as well.
When I use `cv::cuda::split` with an `std::vector<cv::cuda::GpuMat>` it appears to work fine.
My 3 channel float matrix is split as expected; however when I try the other function overload which uses a single `cv::cuda::GpuMat` I get a segmentation fault.
I'm basically trying to split a float matrix into a 3 channel BGR matrix:
// mfloat has the float data
cv::cuda::GpuMat mfloat; // <-- this has already been populated with data
cv::cuda::GpuMat input_matrix;
cv::cuda::split(mfloat, &input_matrix);
This SEGFAULTs with:
```
Thread 1 "test_computer_v" received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.
0x00007fffead6844d in cv::cuda::GpuMat::create(int, int, int) () from /usr/local/lib/libopencv_core.so.3.3
```
I've tried setting the matrix size and allocating it, although the documentation does not make it clear what is expected, by setting each row to contain a full-sized matrix `cv::cuda::GpuMat input_matrix(3, 224 * 224, CV_32FC1);` but the same error still persists. Same with `cv::cuda::GpuMat input_matrix(224 * 224, 3, CV_32FC1);`.
When I allocate and pass pointers (std::unique_ptr or a raw pointer) I then get a SEGFAULT from a different line:
cv::cuda::GpuMat * input_matrix = new cv::cuda::GpuMat(224, 224, CV_32FC1);
cv::cuda::split(mfloat, input_matrix);
The error:
```
Thread 1 "test_computer_v" received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.
0x00007fffead6833d in cv::cuda::GpuMat::release() () from /usr/local/lib/libopencv_core.so.3.3
```
Can somebody explain **how** is the version of `split` supposed to be called?
How is `dst` to be allocated and sized?
void cv::cuda::split(InputArray 	src,
GpuMat * 	dst,
Stream & 	stream = Stream::Null()
Many thanks!

title: Python's cv2.cuda.GpuMat non-blocking copyTo() does not copy into destination
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.7.0
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 20.04.5 LTS
Python version: 3.8.10
CUDA 11.8
When using the non-blocking version of GpuMat copyTo() method the contents of the destination GpuMat are not changed.
```
import cv2 as cv
import numpy as np

original = cv.cuda.GpuMat(np.ones((3,3)))
original_roi = original.colRange(1,2).rowRange(1,2)
new_data = cv.cuda.GpuMat(np.zeros((1,1)))

stream = cv.cuda.Stream()
new_data.copyTo(original_roi, stream=stream)
stream.waitForCompletion()

print(original.download())

# output:
# [[1. 1. 1.]
#  [1. 1. 1.]
#  [1. 1. 1.]]
# expected:
# [[1. 1. 1.]
#  [1. 0. 1.]
#  [1. 1. 1.]]
```

title: How to determine object position from image

At the moment I can detect the marbles from a colour picture.
I have access to a depth camera as well and can acquire the distance to the marble this way.
I now need to determine the position of that marble in the 3D space in order to pick it up with a robot arm.
Does anyone have a idea of how I could approach this?

title: compHist with HISTCMP_CORREL method
body:
The imgproc module has a comparison of histogram function that have different methods.
I have used the first HISTCMP_CORREL that calculates the correlation of 2 histograms as it can be seen in the documentation ([link](https://docs.opencv.org/master/d6/dc7/group__imgproc__hist.html#ga994f53817d621e2e4228fc646342d386)).
I have tried to replicate that formula with NumPy to calculate the correlation but the result is far for being equal. In fact, I tried with the formula, with NumPy built-in function and also looking to the source code of OpenCV and trying to replicate it ([link](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/master/modules/imgproc/src/histogram.cpp) from line 1923).
```
import cv2
import numpy as np

def OpenCV_compareHist(H1, H2):
	compareHist = cv2.compareHist(H1, H2, cv2.HISTCMP_CORREL)
	print 'compareHist - OpenCV: ', compareHist

def My_compareHist(H1, H2):
	H1 = H1.flatten()
	H2 = H2.flatten()
	compareHist = np.sum( (H1 - np.mean(H1)) * (H2 - np.mean(H2)) ) / np.sqrt( np.sum((H1 - np.mean(H1))**2) * np.sum((H2 - np.mean(H2))**2) )
	print 'compareHist - Formula: ', compareHist
	print 'compareHist - NumPy: ', np.corrcoef(H1, H2)[0, 1]
	s12 = np.sum(H1*H2)
	s1 = np.sum(H1)
	s11 = np.sum(H1*H1)
	s2 = np.sum(H2)
	s22 = np.sum(H2*H2)
	total = 256.*256.*256.
	scale = 1./total
	num = s12 - s1*s2*scale
	denom2 = (s11 - s1*s1*scale)*(s22 - s2*s2*scale)
	print 'compareHist - CPP: ', num / np.sqrt(denom2)

if __name__ == "__main__":
	img1 = cv2.imread('img_1.jpg', -1)
	img2 = cv2.imread('img_2.jpg', -1)

	H1 = cv2.calcHist([img1],channels=[0,1,2],mask=None,histSize=[256,256,256],ranges=[0,256,0,256,0,256])
	H2 = cv2.calcHist([img2],channels=[0,1,2],mask=None,histSize=[256,256,256],ranges=[0,256,0,256,0,256])

	OpenCV_compareHist(H1, H2)
	My_compareHist(H1, H2)
```
Curiously, the 3 options give me the same value, but the OpenCV `compareHist` function gives another value. How can it be?

title: Pass the AVDictionary generated from OPENCV_FFMPEG_CAPTURE_OPTIONS and OPENCV_FFMPEG_WRITER_OPTIONS to avformat_find_stream_info as well to gain access to more ffmpeg options
body:
While working on https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/22622 i realized that the environment variables ```OPENCV_FFMPEG_CAPTURE_OPTIONS``` and ```OPENCV_FFMPEG_WRITER_OPTIONS``` can only configure a very small subset of the ffmpeg options (the options listed in https://git.ffmpeg.org/gitweb/ffmpeg.git/blob/419d2524a8239a8f00b4c1702c91065b259615a2:/libavutil/opt.c#l465). That prevented me from passing all necessary options to fully specify the stream (https://github.com/opencv/opencv/issues/22622#issuecomment-1279731722).
Anyway i suggest passing the AVDictionary not just to ```avformat_open_input``` but to ```avformat_find_stream_info``` as well https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/a60496f9dfcb2070e1adf935055d1a5b531e3a96/modules/videoio/src/cap_ffmpeg_impl.hpp#L1147 and maybe even to ```avcodec_open2``` https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/a60496f9dfcb2070e1adf935055d1a5b531e3a96/modules/videoio/src/cap_ffmpeg_impl.hpp#L1266

```
  constexpr int size = 64;  
  auto grid1{Mat_<int>(3, array<int, 3>{size, size, size}.data(), 42)};   
  cout << grid1(1, 2, 3) << endl;
```

title: After cv2.event: newline symbol (“\n”) prevents rest of string from printing
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => 3.3.1
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 10 64
- Compiler => Spyder 3.3.1
I have an extensive function that is supposed to print a longer message between a series of interactive mouse events that get triggered in a while loop. My problem is that any line-break symbols \n I use to break up the longer string will stop the rest of the string from printing. The rest of the message gets printed only after the next mouse event, which is rather silly because the message is containing info about what to do next.
I have not encountered such behavior before, don't know whether it is to be expected or what I can do to stop it. All of the string gets printed when I take out the line breaks, but then the returned text becomes unstructured and unintuitive.
I tried `sys.stdout.write()` with `sys.stdout.flush()`, as wel as linebreaks in `""" """` without success.
```
import cv2
import numpy as np

class click_action_func(object):
    def __init__(self):
        print("\nstart program\n")
        self.done_step1 = False
        self.done_step2 = False

    def mouse_step1(self, event, x, y, buttons, user_param):
        if event == cv2.EVENT_LBUTTONDOWN:
            print("mouse action for step 1")
        if event == cv2.EVENT_RBUTTONDOWN:
            self.done_step1 = True
            print("end step 1\n---instructions for step 2---(this should be printed together with end step 1, but wont until the next leftclick)")

    def mouse_step2(self, event, x, y, buttons, user_param):
        if event == cv2.EVENT_LBUTTONDOWN:
            print("mouse action step 2")
        if event == cv2.EVENT_RBUTTONDOWN:
            print("end step 2 \nlong text over \nmultiple lines explaining what you just did")
            self.done_step2 = True

    def run(self, image):
        print("instructions\nleft button: do something in image\nright button: finish step 1, receive instructions for next step\nright button again: end program")
        cv2.namedWindow("window", flags=cv2.WINDOW_NORMAL)
        cv2.setMouseCallback("window", self.mouse_step1)

        while(not self.done_step1):
            cv2.imshow("window", image)
            cv2.waitKey(50)

        cv2.namedWindow("window", flags=cv2.WINDOW_NORMAL)
        cv2.setMouseCallback("window", self.mouse_step2)

        while(not self.done_step2):
            cv2.imshow("window", image)
            cv2.waitKey(50)

        cv2.destroyAllWindows()
        print("\nend program")

img = np.zeros((500, 500))
c = click_action_func()
c.run(img)
```

title: Exceptions when not named parameters
body:
Python version: 3.8.18
OpenCV python version: 4.10.0
Operating System / Platform: Windows-10-10.0.19045-SP0
```
cv2.error: OpenCV(4.10.0) D:\a\opencv-python\opencv-python\opencv\modules\imgproc\src\deriv.cpp:104: error: (-211:One of the arguments' values is out of range) The kernel size must be odd and not larger than 31 in function 'cv::getSobelKernels'
```
```
import numpy as np
 import cv2
  src = (np.random.rand(10, 10, 1) * 256).astype(np.uint8)  
blockSize = 3  
kSize = 3  
borderType = cv2.BORDER_DEFAULT
  output = cv2.cornerMinEigenVal(src, blockSize, kSize, borderType)
```
In this case, it would be throw the exception.
but if use
```
output = cv2.cornerMinEigenVal(src=src, blockSize=blockSize, ksize=kSize, borderType=borderType)
```
it can be correct.
I think it is a bug or error.

title: Morphological Opening not idempotent
body: ##### System information (version) - OpenCV => 4.5.5.64 - Operating System / Platform => Windows 64 Bit - Compiler => OpenCV-python There seems to be a bug with the morphological filter and the borders of an image. As a result applying the same filter with the same kernel multiple times results in different images, even though the operation is supposed to be idempotent. Input image: When trying to remove the skinny rectangles in the top right using MORPH_OPEN, without adding a border to the image manually, the rectangles along the border are not removed. However, if the function is applied multiple times using the same kernel, the rectangles can be removed. This can be solved manually by adding additioal border around the image. Applying the function multiple times using the same kernel should not result in different output images. ```python import cv2 as cv import numpy as np # %matplotlib inline import matplotlib.pyplot as plt matplotlib.rc('image', cmap='gray') # load image img = cv.imread("image.jpg", 0) # apply binary thresholding to circumvent jpg compression img = (img > 128).astype(np.uint8) # adding a border around the image solves the issue # img = cv.copyMakeBorder(img, 1, 1, 1, 1, cv.BORDER_CONSTANT) kernel = cv.getStructuringElement(cv.MORPH_RECT,(20,5)) A = cv.morphologyEx(img, cv.MORPH_OPEN, kernel) B = cv.morphologyEx(A, cv.MORPH_OPEN, kernel) C = cv.morphologyEx(B, cv.MORPH_OPEN, kernel) f, ax = plt.subplots(1,3) ax[0].imshow(A) ax[0].set_title("A") ax[1].imshow(B) ax[1].set_title("B") ax[2].imshow(C) ax[2].set_title("C") plt.show() ``` Resulting images (notice the rectangles along the right border of the image): ![output](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/63684347/172514428-5fe98e1d-da49-4351-80c7-45fa075e95f7.png)

title: cv2.VideoWriter fails to write *.mp4 video files
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.9.0
Operating System / Platform: FreeBSD 14.0
Python version: 3.9
The attached Python fails to write the *.mp4 file, but succeeds to write the *.avi file when the corresponding line is un-commented.
The first of these lines causes the file ```filename.avi``` to be written, but the second line doesn't cause ```filename.mp4``` to be written:
```
#result = cv2.VideoWriter('filename.avi', cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'MJPG'), 25.0, size) 
result = cv2.VideoWriter('filename.mp4', cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'MP4V'), 25.0, size)
```
The second line also produces this additional warning:
```
[ WARN:0@0.074] global cap_gstreamer.cpp:2617 open OpenCV | GStreamer warning: cannot link elements
```
which may or may not be related.
Run this script to capture using the webcam, press S to stop:
```
import cv2 
  
   
# Create an object to read  from camera 
video = cv2.VideoCapture(0) 
   
# We need to check if camera 
# is opened previously or not 
if (video.isOpened() == False):  
    print("Error reading video file") 
  
# We need to set resolutions.  so, convert them from float to integer. 
frame_width = int(video.get(3)) 
frame_height = int(video.get(4)) 
   
size = (frame_width, frame_height) 
   
#result = cv2.VideoWriter('filename.avi', cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'MJPG'), 25.0, size) 
result = cv2.VideoWriter(f'filename.mp4', cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'MP4V'), 25.0, size)

while(True): 
    ret, frame = video.read() 
  
    if ret == True:  
        # Write the frame into the file
        result.write(frame) 
  
        # Display the frame saved in the file 
        cv2.imshow('Frame', frame) 
  
        # Press S on keyboard to stop the process 
        if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('s'): 
            print('exiting ...')
            break

    # Break the loop 
    else: 
        break
  
# When everything done, release the video capture and video write objects 
video.release() 
result.release() 
    
# Closes all the frames 
cv2.destroyAllWindows() 
   
print("The video was successfully saved") 
```

Step 0. Preparation
Drawing on other issues, I fixed some problems that were not suitable for qnx in OpenCV4.7.0 version, and can successfully compile and run (without FFMpeg). But when running on the qnx710 development board, the video file cannot be opened. There is no error message. I analyze that it should be a problem with the underlying videoio codec, and this will not cause an OpenCV error.
Step 1. Cross compile FFMpeg-5.1.4
Below is my ffmpeg compilation script. I successfully compiled the dynamic and static libraries of FFMpeg, and they all meet the minimum version number required for OpenCV compilation. I placed ffmpeg-config.cmake under build/ so that OpenCV can link to it when compiling.
- build.sh of FFMpeg
```bash
source /usr/local/toolchains/qnx710/qnxsdp-env.sh
./configure --prefix=./build --target-os=qnx --arch=aarch64 \
            --cc=/usr/local/toolchains/qnx710/host/linux/x86_64/usr/bin/aarch64-unknown-nto-qnx7.1.0-gcc \
            --cxx=/usr/local/toolchains/qnx710/host/linux/x86_64/usr/bin/aarch64-unknown-nto-qnx7.1.0-g++ \
            --enable-cross-compile --enable-static --enable-shared --disable-doc --disable-asm \
            --disable-ffmpeg --disable-ffplay --disable-ffprobe --enable-avdevice \
            --enable-swscale --disable-postproc --enable-avfilter --enable-pthreads \
            --disable-w32threads --disable-os2threads --disable-d3d11va --disable-dxva2 \
            --disable-vaapi --disable-vdpau --disable-videotoolbox --disable-audiotoolbox \
            --disable-securetransport --disable-amf --disable-cuda-llvm --disable-cuvid \
            --disable-nvenc --disable-nvdec --disable-avisynth --enable-bzlib --disable-iconv \
            --enable-zlib --enable-lzma --disable-sdl2 --enable-gpl --enable-version3 \
            --enable-nonfree --disable-stripping --disable-indevs --disable-libxcb --enable-network \
            --extra-cflags=-fPIC --extra-libs=-lc

make VERBOSE=1 -j8

make install
```
- ffmpeg-config.cmake
```bash
set(ffmpeg_path "${CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR}/ffmpeg-5.1.4/build")

message("ffmpeg_path: ${ffmpeg_path}")

set(FFMPEG_EXEC_DIR "${ffmpeg_path}/bin")
set(FFMPEG_LIBDIR "${ffmpeg_path}/lib")
set(FFMPEG_INCLUDE_DIRS "${ffmpeg_path}/include")

# library names
set(FFMPEG_LIBRARIES
    ${FFMPEG_LIBDIR}/libavcodec.a
    ${FFMPEG_LIBDIR}/libavdevice.a
    ${FFMPEG_LIBDIR}/libavfilter.a
    ${FFMPEG_LIBDIR}/libavformat.a
    ${FFMPEG_LIBDIR}/libavutil.a
    ${FFMPEG_LIBDIR}/libswresample.a
    ${FFMPEG_LIBDIR}/libswscale.a
)

# found status
set(FFMPEG_libavformat_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavdevice_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavcodec_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavutil_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libswscale_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libswresample_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavfilter_FOUND TRUE)

# found status
set(FFMPEG_libavformat_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavdevice_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavcodec_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavutil_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libswscale_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libswresample_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_libavfilter_FOUND TRUE)

# library versions, 注意这几个变量，一定要设置为全局CACHE变量
set(FFMPEG_libavutil_VERSION 57.28.100 CACHE INTERNAL "FFMPEG_libavutil_VERSION") # info
set(FFMPEG_libavcodec_VERSION 59.37.100 CACHE INTERNAL "FFMPEG_libavcodec_VERSION") # info
set(FFMPEG_libavformat_VERSION 59.27.100 CACHE INTERNAL "FFMPEG_libavformat_VERSION") # info
set(FFMPEG_libavdevice_VERSION 59.7.100 CACHE INTERNAL "FFMPEG_libavdevice_VERSION") # info
set(FFMPEG_libavfilter_VERSION 8.44.100 CACHE INTERNAL "FFMPEG_libavfilter_VERSION") # info
set(FFMPEG_libswscale_VERSION 6.7.100 CACHE INTERNAL "FFMPEG_libswscale_VERSION") # info
set(FFMPEG_libswresample_VERSION 4.7.100 CACHE INTERNAL "FFMPEG_libswresample_VERSION") # info

set(FFMPEG_FOUND TRUE)
set(FFMPEG_LIBS ${FFMPEG_LIBRARIES})

status("    #################################### FFMPEG:"       FFMPEG_FOUND         THEN "YES (find_package)"                       ELSE "NO (find_package)")
status("      avcodec:"      FFMPEG_libavcodec_VERSION    THEN "YES (${FFMPEG_libavcodec_VERSION})"    ELSE NO)
status("      avformat:"     FFMPEG_libavformat_VERSION   THEN "YES (${FFMPEG_libavformat_VERSION})"   ELSE NO)
status("      avutil:"       FFMPEG_libavutil_VERSION     THEN "YES (${FFMPEG_libavutil_VERSION})"     ELSE NO)
status("      swscale:"      FFMPEG_libswscale_VERSION    THEN "YES (${FFMPEG_libswscale_VERSION})"    ELSE NO)
status("      avresample:"   FFMPEG_libavresample_VERSION THEN "YES (${FFMPEG_libavresample_VERSION})" ELSE NO)
```
Step 2. Compile OpenCV with FFMpeg
FFMpeg is located in my OpenCV source directory, and I compile it through the build.sh script. Below is my build.sh script.
```bash
source /usr/local/toolchains/qnx710/qnxsdp-env.sh
cmake .. -DTARGET_CPU:STRING=qnx -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=/usr/local/toolchains/cppbuild-1.0.0/assets/cmake/toolchain/linux-aarch64-qnx710.cmake \
            -DCPPBUILD_TARGET_CPU_TYPE:STRING=qnx -DQNXNTO=true \
            -DBUILD_TESTS=OFF -DBUILD_PERF_TESTS=OFF -DWITH_CUDA=OFF -DWITH_VTK=OFF -DWITH_MATLAB=OFF -DBUILD_DOCS=OFF \
            -DBUILD_opencv_python3=OFF -DBUILD_opencv_python2=OFF -DWITH_IPP=OFF -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON \
            -DBUILD_opencv_apps=OFF -DWITH_CUDA=OFF -DWITH_OPENCL=OFF -DWITH_VTK=OFF -DWITH_MATLAB=OFF \
            -DBUILD_DOCS=OFF -DBUILD_JAVA=OFF -DBUILD_FAT_JAVA_LIB=OFF -DWITH_PROTOBUF=OFF -DWITH_QUIRC=OFF \
            -DWITH_FFMPEG=ON -DOPENCV_FFMPEG_USE_FIND_PACKAGE=ON -DOPENCV_FFMPEG_SKIP_BUILD_CHECK=ON \
            -DOPENCV_FFMPEG_ENABLE_LIBAVDEVICE=ON -DFFMPEG_DIR=ffmpeg-5.1.4/build/

make VERBOSE=1 -j8

make install
```
Execute compilation, and then the problem as shown in the title will be reported.

title: Why are dependencies like libjpeg not included in opencv_world?
body:
The `opencv_world` supermodule was introduced in 2012 to allow linking all of the OpenCV modules at once, but one still needs to separately link many common dependencies like `IlmImf`, `libtiff`, `libjpeg` and `libpng`.
Is this a license issue or is there a different reason that these are not included in `opencv_world` or some `opencv_world_and_dependencies` supermodule?

title: where is the jni wrapper
body:
As well known, porting c/c++ library to android need two step, cross compile to achieve shared/static library and then write jni wrapper, which allow us to call c/c++ function with java code.
However, i could not find the jni code and native java function declaration in this repository, expect a little about mat in module/java/generator and also the python script gen_java.py.
So where is the jni wrapper code? is it generated by gen_java.py and then directly link to library ?

title: Building cpp apps with opencv-python package
body:
OpenCV package available in some Linux distros could be outdated and to build a cpp executable with OpenCV one needs to build OpenCV from source (that's a default installation path provided it the [docs](https://docs.opencv.org/4.x/d7/d9f/tutorial_linux_install.html)). At the same time, `opencv-python` package can be utilized as a source of OpenCV binaries to link against.
For instance, one can imagine the following works from CMake:
```cmake

find_package(Python3 REQUIRED)
execute_process(
    COMMAND ${Python3_EXECUTABLE} -c "from cv2.build_utils import get_cmake_path; print(get_cmake_path(), end='')"
    OUTPUT_VARIABLE OpenCV_DIR_PY
    ERROR_QUIET
)

find_package(OpenCV REQUIRED
             COMPONENTS core
             HINTS "${OpenCV_DIR_PY}")
```
In that case `pip install opencv-python` is enough to start building cpp apps with OpenCV.

title: undistort distCoeffs: difference between doc & code
body:
It has come to my attention that the [following term](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/7de627c504a3b8aa16cdc3de56efdc358df4b061/modules/calib3d/src/undistort.dispatch.cpp#L378):
`double icdist = (1 + ((k[7]*r2 + k[6])*r2 + k[5])*r2)/(1 + ((k[4]*r2 + k[1])*r2 + k[0])*r2);`
that can be found inside some distortion functions' internal algorithm on multiple occasions might diverge from [the documentation](https://docs.opencv.org/4.2.0/d9/d0c/group__calib3d.html#ga55c716492470bfe86b0ee9bf3a1f0f7e) where one can read:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/68554285/116961646-e51b8500-ac71-11eb-9a4d-6a63794a2c61.png)
`icdist` is later on multiplied (not divided) with `x`. That is, given `k`, this term seems inverted according to the doc. I was porting some manual code to open-cv and only when I followed the logic of open-cv code order on `k` (rather than the doc order) was open-cv's own function able to correct a test image with the same coefficients I had previously collected and used on the "manual" code.
I could not find this issue elsewhere in this repository and I hope I'm not wasting anyone's time here.

title: wrong fps written in raspberry pi
body:
If I write a video with a non-integer fps in raspberry pi then it does not seem to write the correct fps to the output file. ffprobe reports tbr=9 for 4.5 or 1.8 fps; and tbr=5 for 2.5fps. Integer fps are reported correctly. The same code on laptop with 4.5 fps outputs a file with tbr=4.5 reported by ffprobe on laptop and rpi.
A second issue is that despite this vlc manages to play the output at correct speed. So how does it detect the correct fps?
Example code:
```
import cv2
source = "test.avi"
reader = cv2.VideoCapture(source)
writer = cv2.VideoWriter("temp.avi", cv2.VideoWriter_fourcc(*'DIVX'), 4.5, (800, 600))
while True:
    res, img = reader.read()
    if not res:
        break
    writer.write(img)
reader.release()
writer.release()
```

title: OpenCV Truck Factor
body:
As part of my PhD research on code authorship, we calculated the Truck Factor (TF) of some popular GitHub repositories.
As you probably know, the [Truck (or Bus) Factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_factor) designates the minimal number of developers that have to be hit by a truck (or quit) before a project is incapacitated. In our work, we consider that a system is in trouble if more than 50% of its files become orphan (i.e., without a main author).
More details on our work in this preprint: https://peerj.com/preprints/1233
We calculated the TF for **OpenCV** and obtained a value of **2**.
The developers responsible for this TF are:
Andrey Pavlenko - author of 55% of the files
Vladislav Vinogradov - author of 28% of the files
To validate our results, we would like to ask **OpenCV** developers the following three brief questions:
(a) Do you agree that the listed developers are the main developers of **OpenCV**?
(b) Do you agree that **OpenCV** will be in trouble if the listed developers leave the project (e.g., if they win in the lottery, to be less morbid)?
(c) Does **OpenCV** have some characteristics that would attenuate the loss of the listed developers (e.g., detailed documentation)?
Guilherme Avelino
PhD Student
Applied Software Engineering Group (ASERG)
UFMG, Brazil
http://aserg.labsoft.dcc.ufmg.br/

title: Adding new functionality to cv::warpPolar
body:
`cv::warpPolar` pays too much attention to the central region and ignores information about the edges of the image when mapping the image from Cartesian space to polar coordinate space.
I introduced a new radial non-uniform sampling method, i.e., I introduced new macro definitions WARP_POLAR_EXP and WARP_POLAR_SQUARE to compensate for the loss of edge information. A [pull request](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/pull/26681) has been submitted, please merge the contents.
For case examples, see [example](https://github.com/shuaikaishi/RRE).

title: All different shapes of circles depending on the method
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.7.0
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 18.04.6 LTS
Python version: 3.8.16
Have you ever noticed that the circles drawn with OpenCV are all different depending on the method? Is this a bug?
![image](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/assets/6425237/6f85d0e7-b2f6-4768-97bc-319ce70ed7f0)
```python
import cv2
import numpy as np


def create_kernel_v1(radius):
    """Using cv2.circle()"""

    diameter = radius * 2 + 1
    image = np.zeros((diameter, diameter), np.uint8)
    cv2.circle(image, (radius, radius), radius, 1, -1)

    return image


def create_kernel_v2(radius):
    """Using cv2.ellipse()"""

    diameter = radius * 2 + 1
    image = np.zeros((diameter, diameter), np.uint8)
    cv2.ellipse(image, (radius, radius), (radius, radius), 0, 0, 360, 1, -1)

    return image


def create_kernel_v3(radius):
    """Using cv2.getStructuringElement()"""
    diameter = radius * 2 + 1
    kernel = cv2.getStructuringElement(cv2.MORPH_ELLIPSE, (diameter, diameter))
    return kernel


def main():

    for radius in (1, 2, 3, 4, 5):

        image1 = create_kernel_v1(radius)
        image2 = create_kernel_v2(radius)
        image3 = create_kernel_v3(radius)

        result_1vs2 = np.array_equal(image1, image2)
        result_2vs3 = np.array_equal(image2, image3)

        print(radius, image1.shape, result_1vs2, result_2vs3, sep='\t')
        # 1  (3, 3)    True   True
        # 2  (5, 5)    True   False
        # 3  (7, 7)    False  False
        # 4  (9, 9)    False  False
        # 5  (11, 11)  False  False

        cv2.imwrite(f'radius{radius}_v1.png', image1 * 255)
        cv2.imwrite(f'radius{radius}_v2.png', image2 * 255)
        cv2.imwrite(f'radius{radius}_v3.png', image3 * 255)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

Test code:
```
import cv2


def mouse_callback(event, x, y, flags, param):
    if event == cv2.EVENT_LBUTTONDBLCLK:
        print(x, y)


cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
if not cap.isOpened():
    print("Couldn't open camera")

while True:
    ret, frame = cap.read()
    cv2.setMouseCallback('frame', mouse_callback)
    cv2.imshow('frame', frame)
    if cv2.waitKey(10) & 0xFF == ord("q"):
        break

```
- [✔️  ] I report the issue, it's not a question
- [ ✔️ ] I checked the problem with documentation, FAQ, open issues,
forum.opencv.org, Stack Overflow, etc and have not found any solution
- [✔️  ] I updated to the latest OpenCV version and the issue is still there
- [ ✔️ ] There is reproducer code and related data files: videos, images, onnx, etc

body:
The OpenCV build process fails when doing make -j16 on my computer
Hardware specs:

Ram: 16GB
GPU: RTX 3060
Software specs:

I downloaded the following packages from apt:

title: VideoCapture's index of webcam changes when using rasberry pi
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.7.0.68
Operating System: Windows 11
Python version: 3.10
When running the code to take a videocapture
```
import cv2
cam = cv2.VideoCapture(0)
```
I wondered if there was a way to always access the webcam of any laptop with the same code, because the index used differs depending on the laptop that runs the code.
For some, its when the index is 0, for others its 1, we even had one where the webcam was the index was 4.
I dont know if it's actually possible but it would be great.
By having an external camera and trying to access the webcam in different OS.

title: core: performance test OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide fails on ARM
body:
##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => master (8213e57f3b032033b6c4c1e3eb3bdc84c12c0e1a)
- Operating System / Platform => ARM 32bit (Jetson TK1)
- Compiler => gcc 4.8.4
##### Summary of the issue
- Divide using NEON is too aggressive of losing precision
- I'd like to ask a suggestion of way to fix it
- opencv_perf_core fails on OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide
```
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/0, where GetParam() = (640x480, 8UC1)
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/2, where GetParam() = (640x480, 8UC3)
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/4, where GetParam() = (640x480, 8UC4)
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/6, where GetParam() = (1280x720, 8UC1)
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/8, where GetParam() = (1280x720, 8UC3)
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/10, where GetParam() = (1280x720, 8UC4)

[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/16, where GetParam() = (1920x1080, 8UC4)

[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/20, where GetParam() = (3840x2160, 8UC3)
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/22, where GetParam() = (3840x2160, 8UC4)
```
- The failure is because the values are slightly different (expected value was **2** when the actual value was **1**)
```
[ RUN      ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/0
/home/ubuntu/opencv-fork/modules/ts/src/ts_perf.cpp:377: Failure
The difference between expect_last and actual_last is 1, which exceeds eps, where
expect_last evaluates to 2,
actual_last evaluates to 1, and
eps evaluates to 0.00025499999999999996.
Argument "dst" has unexpected value of the last element

params    = (640x480, 8UC1)
termination reason:  unknown
bytesIn   =     614400
bytesOut  =     307200
samples   =         13 of 100
outliers  =          1
frequency = 1000000000
min       =     640664 = 0.64ms
median    =     644664 = 0.64ms
gmean     =     644412 = 0.64ms
gstddev   = 0.00548082 = 0.02ms for 97% dispersion interval
mean      =     644421 = 0.64ms
stddev    =       3539 = 0.00ms
[  FAILED  ] OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide/0, where GetParam() = (640x480, 8UC1) (27 ms)
```
- I digged in a bit, and OCL_DivFixture goes to [here ](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/8213e57f3b032033b6c4c1e3eb3bdc84c12c0e1a/modules/core/src/arithm.cpp#L3157-L3165)
```
void div8u( const uchar* src1, size_t step1, const uchar* src2, size_t step2,
                   uchar* dst, size_t step, int width, int height, void* scale)
{
CALL_HAL(div8u, cv_hal_div8u, src1, step1, src2, step2, dst, step, width, height, *(const double*)scale
```
- and this function will call `div` in `carotene` which is [here](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/8213e57f3b032033b6c4c1e3eb3bdc84c12c0e1a/3rdparty/carotene/src/div.cpp#L127)
```
template <typename T>
void div(const Size2D &size,
         const T * src0Base, ptrdiff_t src0Stride,
         const T * src1Base, ptrdiff_t src1Stride,
         T * dstBase, ptrdiff_t dstStride,
         f32 scale,
         CONVERT_POLICY cpolicy)
{
internal::assertSupportedConfiguration();
```
- and calls the function `divSaturateQ` which is [here](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/8213e57f3b032033b6c4c1e3eb3bdc84c12c0e1a/3rdparty/carotene/src/div.cpp#L65)
```
{ return vcvtq_s32_f32(vmulq_f32(vmulq_n_f32(vcvtq_f32_s32(v1), scale), internal::vrecpq_f32(vcvtq_f32_s32(v2)))); }
```
- Now the real cause is that the divide is computed by [reciprocal value ](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/blob/8213e57f3b032033b6c4c1e3eb3bdc84c12c0e1a/3rdparty/carotene/src/intrinsics.hpp#L53)
```
inline float32x4_t vrecpq_f32(float32x4_t val)
{
    float32x4_t reciprocal = vrecpeq_f32(val);
    reciprocal = vmulq_f32(vrecpsq_f32(val, reciprocal), reciprocal);
    reciprocal = vmulq_f32(vrecpsq_f32(val, reciprocal), reciprocal);
    return reciprocal;
}
```
- Approach of using Newton-Raphson method with reciprocal is quite useful to accelerate, but it causes difference in some cases.
- I wrote a [small program](https://github.com/tomoaki0705/sampleReciprocalNeon) to verify that, and based on this program, more than 200 cases causes difference in range of `char` (i.e. -128---128)
- The `actual` value below shows the result using `vrecpeq` and `expected` show the result from normal float operation and then rounded to int.
```
iteration:2 expected:1 actual:0 -126/-126
iteration:2 expected:1 actual:0 -125/-125
iteration:2 expected:2 actual:1 -126/-63
iteration:2 expected:3 actual:2 -126/-42
iteration:2 expected:6 actual:5 -126/-21
iteration:2 expected:-6 actual:-5 -126/21
iteration:2 expected:-3 actual:-2 -126/42
iteration:2 expected:-2 actual:-1 -126/63
iteration:2 expected:-1 actual:0 -125/125
iteration:2 expected:-1 actual:0 -126/126
iteration:2 expected:1 actual:0 -122/-122
iteration:2 expected:1 actual:0 -121/-121
iteration:2 expected:2 actual:1 -122/-61
```
- Now, I think there are three ways to fix this issue
1. loosen the threshold on performance test of division
2. deactivate the NEON optimization just for division
3. avoid including this test case on ARM
- In the past, I was suggested to loosen the threshold only for ARM platform, but in this case, I don't agree about loosing the threshold.  The result differs in int, not float.
- At least for division on integer types, I propose to deactivate any NEON optimization on this.
- Run `opencv_perf_core --gtest_filter=OCL_DivFixture_Divide.Divide*`

title: Add `DFT_COLS` to `cv::DftFlags`
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
`cv::dft` accepts a  `DFT_ROWS` flag (and `DCT_ROWS`) which helps a bit to process a single dimension of n-dimension data. However, in most cases, you still need to do a costly rearrangement of pixels to bring the dimension you are interested in to the row dimension, using `transpose` or similar.
Could you consider adding a `DFT_COLS` flag that would avoid this rearrangement ?

title: cv2.getOptimalNewCameraMatrix did not give correct new camera intrinsics.
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.4.0
Operating System / Platform: Ubuntu 18.04
Python version: 2.7
Camera: IMX219 120° fov
I use ```cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap``` and ```cv2.remap``` to undistort an image, and get new camera intrinsics matrix by using ```cv2.getOptimalNewCameraMatrix```.
For 1920\*1080 picture, it works very well and I can get right results.
However when I tried 2560\*1920 picture, it shows:
raw image:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/69149591/198340536-4baaa5fb-bc09-4614-9cac-ce06d2897319.png)
undistort image:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/69149591/198340708-853d286e-db38-44e2-bf0a-7b0d5515d0c4.png)
It seems not a good result. Usually newCameraMatrix has a little difference to raw camera intrinsics matrix, so I tried to change
```mapx,mapy=cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap(mtx,dist,np.eye(3),newCameraMtx,(w_raw,h_raw),cv2.CV_32FC1)```
to
```mapx,mapy=cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap(mtx,dist,np.eye(3),mtx,(w_raw,h_raw),cv2.CV_32FC1)```, just substitude newCameraMtx with raw mtx, it shows:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/69149591/198342433-cda5d41a-79b9-4dc5-aaef-b4462cc115eb.png)
It is a right result, but not perfect.
So i checked mtx and newCameraMtx, as is shown below.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/69149591/198342845-8c19b5ef-a1fa-4260-9f99-41462e891dbd.png)
and newCameraMtx is:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/69149591/198342986-aa3feaf5-dbae-490d-9cb5-15d742be26c7.png)
They differ a lot. So I think there may exist a bug in function ```cv2.getOptimalNewCameraMatrix```. It didn't give the right new intrinsics.
I also tried ```cv2.undistort``` function. Both  ```cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap```&```cv2.remap``` and ```cv2.undistort``` function show the same result.
raw intrinsics and distort data
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import numpy as np

w_raw,h_raw=2560,1920

              [ 0.               , 0.                , 1.                ]])

newCameraMtx,roi=getOptimalNewCameraMatrix(mtx,dist,(w_raw,h_raw),1,(w_raw,h_raw))
```

title: Enabling OpenEXR after python module import
body:
**Problem**
The current method to enable OpenEXR image reading in python is to add a line with `os.environ["OPENCV_IO_ENABLE_OPENEXR"] = "1"`  before the import of opencv using `import cv2`.
This goes against the pep8 guideline that "Imports are always put at the top of the file, just after any module comments and docstrings, and before module globals and constants." (see https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/#imports).
As a consequence linters such as ruff,  pycodestyle or flake8 generate errors .
One can add comments to ignore the specific linting error in the lines following the line that contains ` os.environ["OPENCV_IO_ENABLE_OPENEXR"] = "1"` but this make the linting weaker and pollutes the code with comments to ignore the errors.
It would be great if we could import opencv first using  `import cv2` and then enable OpenEXR within the code following the imports section using a method `cv2.enable_openexr()` for example.

```
>  - fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti() takes at most 7 arguments (8 given)
>  - fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti() takes at most 7 arguments (8 given)
>  - Can't parse 'h'. Input argument doesn't provide sequence protocol
>  - Can't parse 'h'. Input argument doesn't provide sequence protocol
```
when reading the function signature in VSCode it includes a version of fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti that takes 8 arguments
```
def fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti(
    srcImgs: Sequence[MatLike],
    imgToDenoiseIndex: int,
    temporalWindowSize: int,
    h: Sequence[float],
    dst: MatLike | None = ...,
    templateWindowSize: int = ...,
    searchWindowSize: int = ...,
    normType: int = ...
) -> MatLike: ...
```
When calling with
```
cv.fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti(imgs, idx, temporalWindowSize, h, normType=cv.NORM_L1) #removing optional arguments except for keyword arg normType
```
raises
```
cv2.error: OpenCV(4.9.0-dev) :-1: error: (-5:Bad argument) in function 'fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti'
> Overload resolution failed:
>  - 'normType' is an invalid keyword argument for fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti()
>  - 'normType' is an invalid keyword argument for fastNlMeansDenoisingMulti()
>  - Can't parse 'h'. Input argument doesn't provide sequence protocol
>  - Can't parse 'h'. Input argument doesn't provide sequence protocol
```

title: FaceRecognizer missing documentation for Python

I guess the Face Recognizer page @ http://docs.opencv.org/2.4/modules/contrib/doc/facerec/facerec_api.html is missing documentation for Python. I even tried `>> help(cv2.createLBPHFaceRecognizer)` in the Python console, which resulted in a `AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'createLBPHFaceRecognizer'` error. Could this be looked into? I believe that atleast source documentation should be available, leave alone the web documentation.

title: Performance improvement. Loading video streams into AMD GPU. 2x faster!
body:
Currently opencv loaded images from a MPEG4 stream into a BGR Mat.
To get this onto the AMD GPU, one coverts this to a BGR UMat.
This then sends the image over the PCI bus to the AMD GPU.
This is slow because of the large quantity of data needing to be transferred.
MPEG4 is natively in YCrCb  in 4:2:0  for most data sources.
If we could send this 4:2:0 image to the GPU, it would halve the quantity of data being transferred across the PCI bus, thus improving the performance by 100% (2x quicker)
I have implemented this method for myself, but wondered if anyone else would be interested if I submitted it for review.
Previous BGR method: 150 FPS,  With 420 method, 300 FPS.
The API is quite simple:
UMat input;
UMat input_bgr;
VideoCapture d_reader("/mnt/tmp/test.mkv");
d_reader.set(CAP_PROP_FRAME_FORMAT, 2);  // New property. (2 for 4:2:0, 0 for default BGR) So, backwards compatible.
d_reader.read(input);   // 4:2:0 image now on the GPU.
cv::cvtColor(input, input_bgr, COLOR_YUV2BGR_I420);  // GPU converts it to BGR
For BGR, the processing is as follows:
Media File -> 420 on CPU ->(CPU intensive)  BGR on CPU -> PCI bus (expensive) -> BGR on GPU
For 420, the processing is as follows:
Media File -> 420 on CPU -> PCI Bus (less expensive) -> 420 on GPU -> BGR on GPU (less expensive)
As a general comment, it would all be a lot easier if we could introduce a Planar format.
I.e. a Structure that hold 4 pointers to each of the planes, E.g. one for Blue, one for Green, one for Red, one for Alpha. We could then also attach a type to it, e.g. BGRA, or I420.
We could then transfer all the different formats from ffmpeg to GPU without any CPU memcopy.

Hello.
Recently I was following the original [paper](http://sdpy.googlecode.com/svn/tags/temp/unsorted3/to_delete/tmp/unsorted/to_remove/research/my_papers/phdthesis/review/survey/MB-LBP/fulltext.pdf) about the gentle adaboost training.
I was a little bit confused by the fact that in the paper they use multi-branch tree with 256 values, but the implementation of `traincascade` and [cascade xml files](https://github.com/Itseez/opencv/blob/master/data/lbpcascades/lbpcascade_frontalface.xml#L34) have a two branch tree. Is it a problem or a special trick. I didn't find any information in documentation. Thank you.

title: HoughCircle Algorithm
body:
HoughCircles uses this algorithm currently:
1.Initialize a 2d accumulator matrix.
2.Compute gradient image using sobel derivative.
3.For each edge pixel, only vote normal to the local gradient direction
4.Select the local maxima points which have votes>vote_thres.
We have identified the circle centers and now we need to determine their radius.
5.For each circle center, create histogram of points at every radius. Select (global)maxima as radius if histogram height at maxima>vote_thres.
The obvious flaw is that function detects only the larger circle in case of concentric circles. Why isn't every local maxima above vote_thres taken as the radius?
It would be helpful if someone could direct me any discussions on this topic.I have read the referenced method but it's doesn't specify the algorithm to select radius.

##### System information (version)
- OpenCV => '3.4.3'
- Operating System / Platform => Windows 10 64 Bit

Please update the documentation on [this page](https://docs.opencv.org/3.0-beta/doc/py_tutorials/py_gui/py_image_display/py_image_display.html)
It says
> Warning Even if the image path is wrong, it won’t throw any error, but print img will give you None
However, if the path is **correct** print(img) will return None and we can test against it so below code enters into the if condition.
```.py
    
import cv2

img_path = "E:\data\Licence.png"
retval = cv2.imread(img_path)

if print(retval) is None:
        cv2.imshow('image', retval)
        cv2.waitKey(0)
        cv2.destroyAllWindows()

else:
        print(retval)

```

AttributeError: module 'cv2.ximgproc' has no attribute 'thinning' for python file
body:
Platform: PyCharm 2020.3.5
Python version: 3.7
opencv-contrib-python: 4.10.0.84
opencv-python: 4.10.0.84
opencv-python-headless: 4.10.0.84
D:\Anaconda\envs\car\python.exe D:/transfuser-2022/detection/TDetect.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "D:/transfuser-2022/detection/TDetect.py", line 136, in <module>
result = detector.detect("road_map.png")
File "D:/transfuser-2022/detection/TDetect.py", line 94, in detect
skeleton = self._preprocess(img_path)
File "D:/transfuser-2022/detection/TDetect.py", line 44, in _preprocess
skeleton = cv2.ximgproc.thinning(morph)
AttributeError: module 'cv2.ximgproc' has no attribute 'thinning'
Process finished with exit code 1
import cv2
import numpy as np
from math import degrees, acos, hypot
from collections import defaultdict
class EnhancedTjunctionDetector:
def __init__(self,
road_gray_min=80,
road_gray_max=200,
morph_kernel=5,
canny_thresh=(30, 150),
hough_thresh=40,
min_line_length=80,
max_line_gap=25,
angle_tol=5):
self.road_gray_range = (road_gray_min, road_gray_max)
self.morph_kernel = np.ones((morph_kernel, morph_kernel), np.uint8)
self.canny_thresh = canny_thresh
self.hough_params = (hough_thresh, min_line_length, max_line_gap)
self.angle_tolerance = angle_tol
def _preprocess(self, img_path):
img = cv2.imread(img_path)
gray = cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
blurred = cv2.GaussianBlur(gray, (5, 5), 0)
road_mask = cv2.inRange(blurred, *self.road_gray_range)
morph = cv2.morphologyEx(road_mask, cv2.MORPH_CLOSE, self.morph_kernel)
skeleton = cv2.ximgproc.thinning(morph)
return skeleton
def _detect_segments(self, processed_img):
edges = cv2.Canny(processed_img, *self.canny_thresh)
lines = cv2.HoughLinesP(edges, 1, np.pi / 180, *self.hough_params)
segments = []
if lines is not None:
for line in lines:
x1, y1, x2, y2 = line[0]
segments.append(((x1, y1), (x2, y2)))
return segments
def _vector_angle(self, v1, v2):
dot = v1[0] * v2[0] + v1[1] * v2[1]
mag1 = hypot(v1[0], v1[1])
mag2 = hypot(v2[0], v2[1])
if mag1 * mag2 < 1e-6:
return 180.0
cos_theta = dot / (mag1 * mag2)
return degrees(acos(np.clip(cos_theta, -1.0, 1.0)))
def _is_t_junction(self, vectors):
for i in range(len(vectors)):
for j in range(i + 1, len(vectors)):
angle = self._vector_angle(vectors[i], vectors[j])
if angle < (180 - self.angle_tolerance):
continue
for k in range(len(vectors)):
if k in (i, j):
continue
angle_ik = self._vector_angle(vectors[i], vectors[k])
angle_jk = self._vector_angle(vectors[j], vectors[k])
if (abs(90 - angle_ik) <= self.angle_tolerance or
abs(90 - angle_jk) <= self.angle_tolerance):
return True
return False
def detect(self, img_path):
skeleton = self._preprocess(img_path)
segments = self._detect_segments(skeleton)
connection_map = defaultdict(list)
for seg in segments:
pt1, pt2 = seg
connection_map[pt1].append(pt2)
connection_map[pt2].append(pt1)
for point, neighbors in connection_map.items():
if len(neighbors) < 3:
continue
vectors = []
for neighbor in neighbors:
dx = neighbor[0] - point[0]
dy = neighbor[1] - point[1]
if dx == 0 and dy == 0:
continue
vectors.append((dx, dy))
if len(vectors) >= 3 and self._is_t_junction(vectors):
return True
return False
if __name__ == "__main__":
detector = EnhancedTjunctionDetector(
road_gray_min=80,
road_gray_max=200,
morph_kernel=7,
canny_thresh=(30, 150),
hough_thresh=40,
min_line_length=80,
angle_tol=8
result = detector.detect("road_map.png")
print(f"There exists T road: {result}")

title: AffineFeature Mask
body:
OpenCV python version: 4.6.0
Operating System / Platform: macOS Monterey 12.6
Python version: 3.9.13
I am used to use mask feature detector in OpenCV and it works fine for all except AffineFeature, i.e. once I determine a mask for AffineFeature with any backbone, I see detected points in the area with 0 label in mask.
```python
import cv2 as cv
import numpy as np

I = cv.imread(any image, 0)
MASK = np.zeros_like(I, np.uint8)
MASK[:250, :250] = 1

affinefeature = cv.AffineFeature_create(cv.SIFT_create())
kp, desc = affinefeature.detectAndCompute(I, mask=MASK)
cv.drawKeypoints(I, kp, None, color=-1, flags=2)
# NOTE: based on the defined MASK, we should not have any ASIFT points outside of the MASK (0->250 and 0->250), but there are points there.
```

title: Om object track
body:
import cv2
import numpy as np
# Replace 'path_to_video' with the path to your video file
cap = cv2.VideoCapture('https://github.com/opencv/opencv/assets/169485743/5cf5d70b-cf55-449d-9c4c-02252d81cf98
# Set up the parameters for ShiTomasi corner detection
feature_params = dict(maxCorners=100, qualityLevel=0.3, minDistance=7, blockSize=7)
# Parameters for lucas kanade optical flow
lk_params = dict(winSize=(15, 15), maxLevel=2, criteria=(cv2.TERM_CRITERIA_EPS | cv2.TERM_CRITERIA_COUNT, 10, 0.03))
# Take the first frame and find corners in it
ret, old_frame = cap.read()
old_gray = cv2.cvtColor(old_frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
p0 = cv2.goodFeaturesToTrack(old_gray, mask=None, **feature_params)
# Create a random color to draw tracks
color = np.random.randint(0, 255, (100, 3))
while True:
# Read the new frame
ret, frame = cap.read()
if not ret:
break
frame_gray = cv2.cvtColor(frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
# Calculate optical flow
p1, st, err = cv2.calcOpticalFlowPyrLK(old_gray, frame_gray, p0, None, **lk_params)
# Select good points
good_new = p1[st == 1]
good_old = p0[st == 1]
# Draw the tracks
for i, (new, old) in enumerate(zip(good_new, good_old)):
a, b = new.ravel()
c, d = old.ravel()
frame = cv2.line(frame, (a, b), (c, d), color[i].tolist(), 2)
frame = cv2.circle(frame, (a, b), 5, color[i].tolist(), -1)
# Display the result
cv2.imshow('Frame', frame)
# Update the previous frame and previous points
old_gray = frame_gray.copy()
p0 = good_new.reshape(-1, 1, 2)
if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'):
break
# Release the capture and close any open windows
cap.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()

title: cv::hasNonZero()
body:
### Descripe the feature and motivation
Currently, checking if a matrix is all-zeros can be done with `(cv::countNonZeros(m)==0)`
If the exact number of nz values is irrelevant, some `cv::hasNonZeros()` would stop parsing the matrix at first non-zero value, for a performance gain.
Use cases :
- a mask is all zero and could be released instead of propagated in function calls
- checking if at least one pixel pass a predicate after some global function like `cv::compare()`
I have submitted a PR : [#22947](https://github.com/opencv/opencv/pull/22947)
